name: Kung Pao Chicken

ingredients.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps: Add the pepper and soy sauce.
Fold in the peanuts at the end.

ingredients_full.note: cubed - traditional kung pao uses dark meat, which stays juicier through the high-heat stir-fry than breast

ingredients_full.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps_detailed: Marinate the chicken briefly in half the soy sauce and the cornstarch.
Cook the jasmine rice separately, 15-18 minutes.
Heat oil in a wok over high heat until nearly smoking. Add the chili powder and let it bloom in the oil for 10-15 seconds.
Add the chicken and stir-fry 3-4 minutes until just cooked through and lightly charred at the edges.
Add the ginger and garlic, stir-frying 20-30 seconds until fragrant.
Add the pepper and stir-fry 1-2 minutes until just tender-crisp.
Add the remaining soy sauce and the vinegar, tossing to coat and reduce slightly, 30-45 seconds.
Off the heat, fold in the roasted peanuts.
Top with the scallions and serve over the rice.
description: Gong bao ji ding, a Sichuan classic built on chicken, dried chilies, and peanuts. Real Sichuan peppercorn gives it málà, the tingling-numbing sensation the dish is known for, which chili powder alone can't replicate. Peanuts, not almonds, are what the dish is actually built on.

name: Beef and Broccoli
steps: Toss together with the oyster sauce.

ingredients_full.note: sliced very thin against the grain

steps_detailed: Slice the beef as thin as you can manage, against the grain.
Marinate the beef briefly in the cornstarch and half the soy sauce.
Cook the jasmine rice separately, 15-18 minutes.
Heat a wok until nearly smoking. Sear the beef in a single layer, undisturbed, 60-90 seconds per side, until well browned but still pink in the center; remove.
Add the garlic and ginger to the same wok, 15-20 seconds, then the broccoli, stir-frying 2-3 minutes until bright green and just tender-crisp.
Return the beef to the wok with the oyster sauce and remaining soy sauce, tossing 30-45 seconds until glossy and everything is coated.
Serve over the rice.
description: A home-style stir-fry standard, served over white rice - brown rice isn't traditional here. Shaoxing rice wine, normally used in the marinade for real depth, is left out rather than faked with something that would taste wrong.

name: Shrimp Lo Mein
steps: Toss together with the soy sauce.

ingredients_full.ingredient: McCormick Sesame Oil, Aceite De Atonjoli

steps_detailed: Boil the egg noodles until just tender, 3-4 minutes, then drain and toss with a little oil to prevent sticking.
Heat a wok over high heat. Stir-fry the shrimp 2-3 minutes until just pink; remove.
Add the garlic, carrot, and bok choy, stir-frying 2 minutes until the vegetables are tender-crisp.
Add the noodles, soy sauce, and oyster sauce, tossing everything together over high heat 1-2 minutes until the noodles are coated and starting to catch slightly at the edges.
Return the shrimp, toss once more, and finish with the sesame oil off the heat.
description: Lo mein means 'tossed noodles' and is a wheat-and-egg noodle dish. Rice noodles are for something like pad thai, and behave completely differently in the pan - egg noodles are the correct noodle here.

name: Mapo Tofu
steps: Add the soy sauce, chili powder, and water.
steps_detailed: Cook the jasmine rice separately, 15-18 minutes.
Heat oil in a wok over medium-high heat. Brown the pork, breaking it apart into small crumbles, 4-5 minutes, until the fat renders and the edges crisp slightly.
Add the garlic and ginger, cooking 20-30 seconds until fragrant.
Stir in the miso paste and chili powder, cooking 30 seconds to bloom the flavors into the oil.
Add the soy sauce and 3/4 cup water, bringing to a simmer.
Gently slide in the tofu cubes and simmer 4-5 minutes, spooning the sauce over rather than stirring hard, so the cubes stay whole.
Whisk the cornstarch with a tablespoon of water and stir in to thicken the sauce, 1 minute.
Top with the scallions and serve over the rice.
description: A Sichuan dish normally built on doubanjiang (fermented broad bean and chili paste) and Sichuan peppercorn for málà numbness. Miso paste (Japanese, not Chinese, used only for its fermented depth) plus chili powder gets into the right general territory but tastes noticeably flatter and less complex than real doubanjiang-based mapo tofu, with no numbing sensation at all.

name: Chicken Fried Rice
steps: Cook the chicken and scramble the eggs.
Toss everything together with the soy sauce.

ingredients_full.note: cooked and cooled, ideally a day ahead

ingredients_full.ingredient: McCormick Sesame Oil, Aceite De Atonjoli

steps_detailed: Cook the chicken through in a hot wok, 5-6 minutes; remove.
Scramble the eggs quickly in the same wok, breaking into small curds; remove.
Add a little more oil, then the cold rice, breaking up any clumps with the back of the spatula, and stir-fry over high heat 4-5 minutes, letting it sit undisturbed for 20-30 seconds at a time.
Add the carrot and peas, stir-frying 1-2 minutes.
Return the chicken and egg, add the soy sauce, and toss everything together 1 minute.
Finish with the sesame oil off the heat and top with the scallions.
description: Swapped brown rice for jasmine - fried rice is built on white rice specifically, cooked and cooled at least several hours or overnight so it fries into separate grains instead of steaming into mush.

name: Orange Chicken
steps_detailed: Dredge the chicken in the cornstarch, dip in the beaten egg, then dredge in cornstarch again for a thicker, craggier coating.
Deep-fry or shallow-fry in the oil at 350F, in batches, 4-5 minutes, until deeply golden and crisp; drain on a rack.
Cook the jasmine rice separately, 15-18 minutes.
Whisk the honey, soy sauce, lime juice, orange zest and juice, and garlic together in a clean pan and simmer 3-4 minutes until reduced and glossy.
Toss the fried chicken in the glaze just before serving.
Serve over the rice.
description: A genuine Chinese-American invention rather than a dish from mainland China - it doesn't have a traditional form to be faithful to, so this aims at doing the dish's own real version properly: battered and fried until actually crisp, which is what holds up under the sticky glaze.

name: Chinese Chicken & Cucumber Jar
steps: Layer the rice, chicken, and cucumber in a jar with the soy sauce.

ingredients_full.ingredient: McCormick Sesame Oil, Aceite De Atonjoli

steps_detailed: Smash the cucumber with the flat of a knife rather than slicing it neatly, then cut into chunks.
Toss the cucumber with the soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, and vinegar.
Layer the rice, chicken, and dressed cucumber in a jar.
description: A simple, portable lunch built on smashed cucumber - smashing rather than slicing breaks the flesh open so it takes on the dressing far better. Swapped brown rice for jasmine.

name: Chinese Beef with Broccoli (Sichuan-Style)
steps: Toss together with the oyster sauce.

ingredients_full.note: sliced very thin against the grain

steps_detailed: Marinate the beef briefly in the cornstarch and soy sauce.
Heat a wok until nearly smoking. Bloom the chili powder and garlic in the oil for 15 seconds.
Sear the beef in a single layer, 60-90 seconds per side; remove.
Stir-fry the broccoli 2-3 minutes until tender-crisp.
Return the beef with the oyster sauce, tossing 30-45 seconds until glossy.
Cook the jasmine rice separately and serve the beef and broccoli over it.
description: A spicier, Sichuan-leaning take on beef and broccoli, genuinely differentiated from the plainer version above rather than a duplicate under a different name. Real Sichuan cooking would fry whole dried chilies in the oil first for real depth; chili powder here gets some of the heat without the same complexity.

name: Sweet and Sour Pork
steps: Simmer the pork with the pepper, pineapple, ketchup, and vinegar.
steps_detailed: Dredge the pork in cornstarch, dip in egg, then dredge in cornstarch again for a thick, craggy coating.
Fry in the oil at 350F, in batches, 4-5 minutes, until deeply golden and cooked through; drain on a rack.
Cook the jasmine rice separately, 15-18 minutes.
Whisk the ketchup, vinegar, brown sugar, and soy sauce together in a separate pan and simmer 2-3 minutes until slightly thickened.
Add the pepper and pineapple to the sauce and simmer 2 minutes to soften slightly.
Toss the fried pork through the sauce just before serving.
Serve over the rice.
description: Gu lao rou in its Cantonese-restaurant form depends on a crisp, battered, deep-fried exterior standing up against a wet sauce, closer to the real dish than simmering the pork in sauce would be. Real versions also lean on more vinegar for a sharper tang than a purely sweet glaze.

name: Chicken and Egg Fried Rice
steps: Scramble the eggs and cook the chicken.
Stir-fry the cold rice with both folded through.

ingredients_full.note: cooked and cooled, ideally a day ahead

ingredients_full.ingredient: McCormick Sesame Oil, Aceite De Atonjoli

steps_detailed: Scramble the eggs quickly, breaking into small curds; remove.
Cook the chicken through in the same wok, 5-6 minutes; remove.
Stir-fry the cold rice over high heat, breaking up clumps, letting it sit 20-30 seconds at a time.
Add the peas, then return the chicken and egg, add the soy sauce, and toss together 1 minute.
Finish with the sesame oil off the heat.
description: The same real fried-rice technique as the plain version - cold, day-old rice fried hard and fast - applied with chicken and egg together.

name: Shrimp and Vegetable Chow Mein
steps: Pan-fry the drained noodles until crisp in spots.

ingredients_full.ingredient: McCormick Sesame Oil, Aceite De Atonjoli

steps_detailed: Boil the noodles until just tender, then drain very well and pat dry.
Heat the oil in a wide pan or wok until shimmering. Spread the noodles in an even layer and let them sit undisturbed 2-3 minutes to develop a crisp, browned bottom before tossing.
Push the noodles aside, stir-fry the shrimp 2 minutes until just pink, then the garlic, carrot, and bean sprouts 1-2 minutes.
Toss everything together with the soy sauce, finishing with the sesame oil off the heat.
description: Chow mein uses the same wheat egg noodle as lo mein, but a different technique - the noodles are pan-fried until crisp in spots, rather than just tossed through the sauce soft. Swapped rice noodles for egg noodles to match.

name: Vegetable Dumpling Bowl
steps: Mix the tofu, cabbage, and carrot with the soy sauce.
Serve as a simple stir-fried bowl, without wrapping into dumplings.

ingredients_full.ingredient: McCormick Sesame Oil, Aceite De Atonjoli

steps_detailed: Squeeze as much water as possible out of the chopped cabbage.
Mix the crumbled tofu, cabbage, carrot, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and sesame oil into a cohesive filling.
Make a simple dough from the flour and hot water, knead until smooth, rest 20 minutes, then roll into small thin rounds.
Place a spoonful of filling in the center of each round, fold in half, and pleat the edge to seal.
Steam over simmering water 8-10 minutes, or pan-fry then steam (potsticker-style: sear the bottoms first, then add water and cover to steam through) for a crisp bottom.
description: An actual dumpling recipe, wrapper and all, rather than a deconstructed bowl of the filling - a dish called dumplings should have the dumpling.

name: Chinese Scallion Pancake with Egg
steps: Mix the flour, scallions, and a little water into a batter.

ingredients_full.ingredient: McCormick Sesame Oil, Aceite De Atonjoli

steps_detailed: Make a simple dough from the flour and warm water, knead until smooth, and rest 20 minutes.
Roll into a thin rectangle, brush generously with the sesame oil, and scatter the scallions evenly across the surface with a pinch of salt.
Roll the dough up tightly into a log, then coil it into a spiral like a cinnamon bun, tucking the end underneath, and rest 10 minutes.
Roll the coiled spiral back out flat into a round, keeping the layers intact.
Pan-fry in the vegetable oil over medium heat, 3-4 minutes per side, until deeply golden and visibly flaky at the edges.
Fry the eggs separately and serve alongside with the soy sauce for dipping.
description: Cong you bing is a laminated dough - rolled thin, brushed with oil and scattered with scallion, then rolled up and coiled before rolling flat again - which builds its flaky, layered interior, rather than a single mixed batter.

name: Sesame Broccoli Snack Cup

ingredients.ingredient: McCormick Sesame Oil, Aceite De Atonjoli

steps: Toss the steamed broccoli with the sesame oil and soy sauce.

ingredients_full.ingredient: McCormick Sesame Oil, Aceite De Atonjoli

steps_detailed: Toss the steamed broccoli with the sesame oil, soy sauce, and garlic.
description: A simple, correctly-dressed blanched vegetable side, the way it's actually done in Chinese home cooking.

name: Five Spice Roasted Chickpeas

ingredients.ingredient: Chickpeas (garbanzo beans, bengal gram), mature seeds, canned, drained solids

steps: Toss the dried chickpeas with the oil and five spice.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Chickpeas (garbanzo beans, bengal gram), mature seeds, canned, drained solids

steps_detailed: Toss the dried chickpeas with the oil and five spice.
Roast at 400F for 25-30 minutes until crisp.
description: A simple roasted snack built on real five spice powder.

name: Chinese Almond Cookies
steps: Work in the flour and almonds.
Shape, top with an almond, and bake at 325F for 16-18 minutes.

ingredients_full.note: chopped, plus whole almonds for topping
for the dough, plus extra beaten for egg wash

steps_detailed: Cream the lard and sugar together until light.
Work in the flour and chopped almonds until it just comes together into a soft dough.
Shape into small balls, press a whole almond into the center of each, and brush with the beaten egg.
Bake at 325F (165C) for 16-18 minutes until golden at the edges.
description: Hup toh soh, a Cantonese bakery cookie traditionally made with lard rather than butter, which gives it a distinctive short, sandy crumble that butter can't quite replicate.

name: Chili Garlic Sauce
steps: Pour hot oil over the garlic and chili powder.
steps_detailed: Put the garlic and chili powder in a heatproof bowl.
Heat the oil in a small pan until just beginning to shimmer and lightly smoke, then pour it directly over the garlic and chili - it should sizzle audibly on contact.
Stir in the vinegar and soy sauce once the sizzling settles.
description: Chinese chili garlic sauces (in the style of Lao Gan Ma) are made by pouring hot oil directly over raw chili flakes and garlic, not by mixing in an already-made hot sauce like sriracha, which is Thai-derived rather than Chinese. That oil-blooming step is what builds real flavor here.

name: Hoisin Glaze
steps_detailed: Whisk the hoisin sauce, soy sauce, garlic, and ginger together.
description: A simple glaze built on real hoisin sauce, a genuine, specific Chinese fermented condiment.

name: Chinese Chicken Salad

ingredients_full.ingredient: McCormick Sesame Oil, Aceite De Atonjoli
Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps_detailed: Toss the cabbage, carrot, and mandarin oranges with the sesame oil, vinegar, and soy sauce.
Top with the shredded chicken and chopped peanuts.
description: Genuinely a Chinese-American restaurant creation rather than a dish native to China, so this aims at doing it well on its own terms - peanuts, the standard topping, instead of sesame seeds alone.

name: Hot and Sour Soup
steps: Simmer the broth with the vinegar and white pepper.
Stir in the egg to form ribbons.

ingredients_full.note: soaked in warm water 20 minutes until softened, then sliced
soaked in warm water 20 minutes until softened, tough ends trimmed, tied in a knot if desired

steps_detailed: Soak the wood ear mushroom and lily buds separately in warm water 20 minutes until softened, then drain, slice the wood ear, and trim the tough ends off the lily buds.
Simmer the broth with the bamboo shoots, wood ear, lily buds, vinegar, white pepper, and soy sauce for 5 minutes.
Whisk the cornstarch with a tablespoon of water and stir into the soup to lightly thicken.
Add the tofu and warm through, 2 minutes.
Drizzle in the beaten egg while stirring the soup gently in one direction to form thin ribbons.
Top with the scallions.
description: Hot and sour soup gets its heat specifically from white pepper, not chili - a sharper, more immediate heat. Wood ear mushroom and dried lily buds are traditionally used in this soup for their distinctive textures: wood ear stays firm and slightly crunchy even after simmering, while lily buds add a chewy, faintly tangy bite.
